original_abstract: Since the introduction of Provincial Reconstruction Teams (PRTs) in Afghanistan, many nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) have argued that blending military and humanitarian missions creates "blurred lines" that jeopardize aid worker safety. These organizations contend that such overlap prevents insurgents from distinguishing between combatants and civilians, making humanitarian staff more frequent targets of attack. Despite the prevalence of this claim in policy circles, empirical validation has remained limited. This study evaluates the impact of PRT presence on NGO security using a panel-corrected standard error regression model covering all 34 Afghan provinces during 2010 and 2011. While initial findings indicate that provinces with PRTs experienced a higher volume of security incidents, a more nuanced analysis reveals that this correlation applies only to teams led by coalition partners other than the United States. In contrast, US-led PRTs did not significantly correlate with increased NGO insecurity. These results challenge the "blurred lines" narrative as a universal explanation for aid worker vulnerability and suggest that the specific operational approaches of different lead nations may play a more decisive role than the mere integration of military and relief work.
humanized_abstract: The presence of Provincial Reconstruction Teams (PRTs) in Afghanistan has been criticized and contended by many nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) who believe the integration of military personnel into aid work creates “blurred lines” that undermine the security of aid work. The criticism runs along the lines that because the work is carried out by civilians, and because of the frequency of visits and the nature of the work, the insurgency is better able to target aid workers and others who have become more visible because of the integration of civilian and military personnel. Although the phrase is often used as explanation, there has been little analysis to test the notion. This study uses panel corrected standard error regression in all 34 provinces of Afghanistan over the period 2010 and 2011 to assess whether there was an increase in security incidents affecting NGOs in provinces where a PRT is stationed. Preliminary data suggests that there was an increase in the number of incidents in PRT provinces but further investigation points toward a more complex picture. Provinces with a non-US PRT had a higher number of incidents that fell within the margin of error, while provinces with a US-led PRT had incidents fell within the margin of error. This points to the “blurred lines” being exaggerated as a general threat for NGOs and that the way a PRT is carried out is as important if not more important in general than the integration of civilian and military personnel.
